---
title: "Learning, Identification, and RL Bridges"
description: "A bridge page showing how control changes when models come from data, why system identification differs from reinforcement learning, and where modern RL reconnects with planning and control."
page-type: application
status: published
update-status: current
level: bridge
topic: control-and-dynamics
prerequisites:
  - /applications/control-and-dynamics/constraints-mpc-and-safe-operation.qmd
learning-goals:
  - "Distinguish system identification, learning-based control, and reinforcement learning."
  - "Explain where learned models or learned policies enter a control pipeline."
  - "Interpret model-based RL as a real bridge back toward planning and control."
  - "Connect application-facing learning language to system identification, Bellman methods, and uncertainty."
applications:
  - control
  - reinforcement-learning
  - system-identification
  - robotics
keywords:
  - system identification
  - reinforcement learning
  - model-based RL
  - learning-based control
  - planning
maintainer: mtuann
last-reviewed: 2026-04-25
source-check-date: 2026-04-25
review-cadence: semiannual
date-modified: last-modified
draft: false
---

## Application Snapshot

Once a reader sees state, feedback, estimation, planning, and constraints, the next question is usually:

`where exactly does learning enter?`

In modern systems work, learning can enter in at least three different places:

- before control, by learning a model from data
- inside control, by learning residuals, uncertainty, or planning components
- as the control policy itself, as in many RL pipelines

This page is the shortest bridge for sorting those possibilities cleanly instead of lumping them all together as “AI control.”

## Problem Setting

Classical control often starts from a model that is already written down:

$$
x_{t+1} = f(x_t, u_t).
$$

But real systems rarely hand you a perfect model for free.

Instead, you may have:

- logged input-output data
- a rough physics model with missing effects
- simulation data but limited real-world data
- a reward or task specification without an accurate dynamics model

That opens three different routes:

1. `System identification`
   Learn a model, then design the controller.

2. `Learning-based control`
   Use learned components inside the control loop.

3. `Reinforcement learning`
   Learn behavior from interaction, often through rewards over trajectories.

## Why This Math Appears

This language reuses several math layers already on the site:

- `Control and Dynamics`: state-space structure still organizes what the system is doing
- `Stochastic Control and Dynamic Programming`: Bellman-style sequential decision ideas reappear in many RL setups
- `Signal Processing and Estimation`: learning from data and operating under uncertainty both depend on filtering, noise models, and hidden-state reasoning
- `Optimization`: fitting models, improving policies, and replanning all become optimization problems
- `Learning Theory`: once models are learned from data, sample efficiency, uncertainty, and distribution shift start to matter

So this page is not a detour away from control. It is the place where control starts touching modern data-driven practice directly.

## Math Objects In Use

- trajectory data
- learned dynamics model
- learned residual or disturbance model
- policy $\pi$
- reward or cost over trajectories
- value function or planner
- uncertainty about model quality

The fastest organizing principle is:

- `system identification` learns the model
- `learning-based control` learns part of the pipeline
- `reinforcement learning` learns behavior from interaction

## A Small Worked Walkthrough

Imagine a quadrotor whose nominal physics model is known, but whose aerodynamic drag and battery effects are not calibrated well.

There are at least three distinct ways learning could enter:

1. `System identification`
   Collect flight data, fit a better dynamics model, then run LQR or MPC on that learned model.

2. `Learning-based control`
   Keep the nominal model, but learn a residual correction term that improves prediction inside the planner.

3. `Reinforcement learning`
   Optimize a policy directly from rollouts using a trajectory reward, possibly without ever producing an explicit identified model.

These routes can look similar from far away, but they answer different questions:

- what is the model?
- how should I use the model?
- do I even need a model explicitly, or only good behavior?

That is why “learning in control” is not one idea. It is a family of different insert points.

## Implementation or Computation Note

Three practical questions help sort almost every method quickly:

1. `What is being learned?`
   A model, a residual, a value function, a policy, or an uncertainty estimate?

2. `Where does the data come from?`
   Passive logs, simulator rollouts, online interaction, or a mix?

3. `How are safety and constraints handled while learning?`
   Through hard feasibility checks, soft penalties, shielding, or only post hoc evaluation?

Use these pages as the strongest follow-on support:

- [Learning-Based Control, System Identification, and RL Bridges](../../topics/advanced/control-and-dynamics/concepts/learning-based-control-system-identification-and-rl-bridges.qmd)
- [Controlled Markov Models, Policies, and Cost Functionals](../../topics/advanced/stochastic-control-and-dynamic-programming/concepts/controlled-markov-models-policies-and-cost-functionals.qmd)
- [Value Iteration, Policy Iteration, and Approximate Dynamic Programming](../../topics/advanced/stochastic-control-and-dynamic-programming/concepts/value-iteration-policy-iteration-and-approximate-dynamic-programming.qmd)
- [Applications > Machine Learning](../machine-learning/index.qmd)

## Failure Modes

- calling every data-driven control method “reinforcement learning”
- treating a learned model as trustworthy without checking where the data came from
- ignoring distribution shift between training rollouts and deployment conditions
- assuming high reward in simulation automatically means safe real-world behavior
- forgetting that system identification plus MPC is often a very different workflow from end-to-end policy learning

## Paper Bridge

- [6.435 / System Identification](https://ocw.mit.edu/courses/6-435-system-identification-spring-2005/) - `First pass` - official MIT anchor for the model-learning side of data-driven control. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [AA228 / Decision Making Under Uncertainty](https://aa228.stanford.edu/) - `Paper bridge` - useful once planning, uncertainty, and RL-style decision making begin to overlap. Checked `2026-04-25`.

## Sources and Further Reading

- [6.435 / System Identification](https://ocw.mit.edu/courses/6-435-system-identification-spring-2005/) - `First pass` - official MIT course hub for learning dynamics from data. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [6.435 lecture notes](https://ocw.mit.edu/courses/6-435-system-identification-spring-2005/pages/lecture-notes/) - `First pass` - direct official notes for the identification viewpoint. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [6.435 syllabus](https://ocw.mit.edu/courses/6-435-system-identification-spring-2005/pages/syllabus/) - `First pass` - concise framing of what identification is trying to solve. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [AA203 / Optimal and Learning-Based Control](https://22-23.bulletin.stanford.edu/courses/2136621) - `Second pass` - official Stanford anchor for modern learning-based control language. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [EE365 / Stochastic Control](https://web.stanford.edu/class/ee365/) - `Second pass` - official Stanford course bridge from sequential decision theory toward RL-style viewpoints. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [EE365 lecture slides](https://web.stanford.edu/class/ee365/lectures.html) - `Second pass` - useful when you want the Bellman and stochastic-control side to feel more concrete. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [AA228 / Decision Making Under Uncertainty](https://aa228.stanford.edu/) - `Bridge outward` - a good Stanford bridge once control, planning, and RL are all in play. Checked `2026-04-25`.
